perturbation.original_text: Late payments shall bear interest at the lesser of: (i) the maximum rate permitted by law, and (ii) the rate of 1.5% per month from the due date until paid.
perturbation.changed_text: Late payments shall bear interest at a fixed rate of 2.0% per month from the due date until paid, regardless of the maximum rate permitted by law.
perturbation.explanation: This change sets a fixed interest rate that might exceed the legally permissible maximum, creating a contradiction and potential unenforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Usury Laws - California Constitution, Article XV, Section 1
perturbation.law_explanation: California Constitution Article XV Section 1 limits interest rates on loans and forbearances. By setting a fixed interest rate without regard to the legal maximum, the contract violates this usury law if the fixed rate exceeds the allowable rate at the time the debt is incurred.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text * California Constitution - CONS ARTICLE XV USURY [SECTION 1- SECTION 1.] ( Article 15 added June 8, 1976, by Prop. 14, clause 35. Res.Ch. 5, 1976. ) SECTION 1. The rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, or on accounts after demand, shall be 7 percent per annum but it shall be competent for the parties to any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action to contract in writing for a rate of interest: (1) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, if the money, goods, or things in action are for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, at a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum; provided, however, that any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action the proceeds of which are used primarily for the purchase, construction or improvement of real property shall not be deemed to be a use primarily for personal, family or household purposes; or (2) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action for any use other than specified in paragraph (1), at a rate not exceeding the higher of (a) 10 percent per annum or (b) 5 percent per annum plus the rate prevailing on the 25th day of the month preceding the earlier of (i) the date of execution of the contract to make the loan or forbearance, or (ii) the date of making the loan or forbearance established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on advances to member banks under Sections 13 and 13a of the Federal Reserve Act as now in effect or hereafter from time to time amended (or if there is no such single determinable rate of advances, the closest counterpart of such rate as shall be designated by the Superintendent of Banks of the State of California unless some other person or agency is delegated such authority by the Legislature). No person, association, copartnership or corporation shall by charging any fee, bonus, commission, discount or other compensation receive from a borrower more than the interest authorized by this section upo
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a fixed interest rate, potentially exceeding the legally permitted maximum as indicated in California Constitution Article XV Section 1. The original text allows for the lesser of the legal maximum or 1.5%, ensuring compliance. The changed text specifies a 2.0% interest rate 'regardless of the maximum rate permitted by law,' directly contradicting the usury law detailed in the scraped law snippet, which limits interest rates. This establishes a clear non-compliance and legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Neither party may assign this Agreement without the other's prior written approval, except by operation of law or in connection with the sale of substantially all of the assets of such party's business or the acquisition of such party by a third party.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party may assign this Agreement under any circumstances, including by operation of law, sale of assets, or acquisition by a third party. This prohibition is absolute and without exception.
perturbation.explanation: This change entirely prohibits assignment, conflicting with the legal principle that certain contract rights are assignable by operation of law (e.g., in bankruptcy or inheritance).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-210 - Delegation of Performance; Assignment of Rights
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-210 addresses the assignability of contract rights, especially in the context of sales of goods. While parties can agree to limit or prohibit assignment, an absolute prohibition that ignores assignments by operation of law (like in bankruptcy) may be deemed unenforceable or against public policy in many jurisdictions, as it unduly restricts the transfer of assets and rights in such legal proceedings.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-210. Delegation of Performance; Assignment of Rights. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-210. Delegation of Performance; Assignment of Rights. (1) A party may perform his duty through a delegate unless otherwise agreed or unless the other party has a substantial interest in having his original promisor perform or control the acts required by the contract . No delegation of performance relieves the party delegating of any duty to perform or any liability for breach. (2) Unless otherwise agreed all rights of either seller or buyer can be assigned except where the assignment would materially change the duty of the other party, or increase materially the burden or risk imposed on him by his contract , or impair materially his chance of obtaining return performance. A right to damages for breach of the whole contract or a right arising out of the assignor's due performance of his entire obligation can be assigned despite agreement otherwise. (3)Unless the circumstances indicate the contrary a prohibition of assignment of "the contract" is to be construed as barring only the delegation to the assignee of the assignor's performance. (4) An assignment of "the contract" or of "all my rights under the contract" or an assignment in similar general terms is an assignment of rights and unless the language or the circumstances (as in an assignment for security) indicate the contrary, it is a delegation of performance of the duties of the assignor and its acceptance by the assignee constitutes a promise by him to perform those duties. This promise is enforceable by either the assignor or the other party to the original contract . (5) The other party may treat any assignment which delegates performance as creating reasonable grounds for insecurity and may without prejudice to his rights against the assignor demand assurances from the assignee (Section 2-609 ). Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction by absolutely prohibiting assignment of the agreement, which conflicts with the UCC § 2-210, which states that rights can be assigned unless otherwise agreed, but even then, a right to damages for breach of the whole contract or a right arising out of the assignor's due performance of his entire obligation can be assigned despite agreement otherwise. The changed text ignores this allowance for assignment, even when legally protected, thus contradicting the law. Furthermore, the law explanation points out that an absolute prohibition that ignores assignments by operation of law (like in bankruptcy) may be deemed unenforceable or against public policy.

perturbation.original_text: Each party hereto expressly consents to the personal jurisdiction of the state and federal courts located in Santa Clara County, California, and expressly waives any defense to any action based on inconvenient forum, choice of venue, lack of personal jurisdiction, sufficiency of service of process or the like.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereto expressly consents to the exclusive personal jurisdiction of the Superior Court of California, Santa Clara County, and irrevocably waives any right to remove an action to federal court, regardless of federal question or diversity jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: This modification attempts to prevent removal to federal court even when federal jurisdiction exists, which contradicts federal law regarding removal jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1441 grants defendants the right to remove certain cases from state court to federal court if the federal court has original jurisdiction. While parties can agree to forum selection clauses, a clause that absolutely prohibits removal to federal court when federal jurisdiction exists may be deemed unenforceable as an attempt to oust the federal courts of their Congressionally mandated jurisdiction.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 28 / PART IV / CHAPTER 89 / § 1441 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 28 USC 1441 : Removal of civil actions Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 28-JUDICIARY AND JUDICIAL PROCEDURE PART IV-JURISDICTION AND VENUE CHAPTER 89-DISTRICT COURTS; REMOVAL OF CASES FROM STATE COURTS Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date §1441. Removal of civil actions (a) Generally .-Except as otherwise expressly provided by Act of Congress, any civil action brought in a State court of which the district courts of the United States have original jurisdiction, may be removed by the defendant or the defendants, to the district court of the United States for the district and division embracing the place where such action is pending
perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly waives the right to remove an action to federal court, even when federal jurisdiction exists. This directly contradicts 28 U.S.C. § 1441, which, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, grants defendants the right to remove cases to federal court if the federal court has original jurisdiction. The modification aims to prevent this removal, creating a legal contradiction because it attempts to override a Congressionally granted right.
